_disclaimer: ALL ITEMS IN THIS FILE ARE SYNTHETIC PLACEHOLDERS, hand-authored to exercise the LSAT Item contract. They are NOT real LSAT items and must NOT be used as study material. Real items must be sourced from official LSAC PrepTests with redistribution licensing (D-SR11). Every item carries SyntheticFlag=SYNTHETIC.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Some critics claim the city's new recycling program is too expensive. But the program has already diverted thousands of tons of waste from landfills, and the long-term savings on landfill fees will more than offset its costs. The program is a wise investment.
items.Stem: Which one of the following most accurately expresses the main conclusion of the argument?
items.ChoiceA: The city's recycling program is a wise investment.
items.ChoiceB: The city's recycling program is too expensive.
items.ChoiceC: Long-term savings on landfill fees will offset the program's costs.
items.ChoiceD: The program has diverted thousands of tons of waste from landfills.
items.ChoiceE: Critics of the recycling program are wrong about everything.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. The final sentence states the author's overall judgment; the other claims support it.
items.WhyWrongB: This is the opposing view the author argues against, not the conclusion.
items.WhyWrongC: This is a supporting premise, not the main point.
items.WhyWrongD: This is a premise offered in support of the conclusion.
items.WhyWrongE: Overstated; the author disputes one claim, not everything critics say.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Regular exercise is often recommended for reducing stress. However, exercising too intensely can actually raise stress-hormone levels in the short term. Therefore, people seeking stress relief should favor moderate exercise over high-intensity workouts.
items.Stem: The main point of the argument is that
items.ChoiceA: intense exercise can raise stress hormones in the short term.
items.ChoiceB: exercise is commonly recommended for reducing stress.
items.ChoiceD: high-intensity workouts are dangerous to one's health.
items.ChoiceE: people seeking stress relief should favor moderate over high-intensity exercise.
items.WhyWrongA: A premise; it supports the recommendation.
items.WhyWrongB: A premise setting up the argument, not its conclusion.
items.WhyWrongC: Too strong; the argument never claims stress is eliminated.
items.WhyWrongD: Too strong; the argument only says intensity raises hormones short-term.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. The 'Therefore' sentence states the recommendation the argument builds toward.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The museum plans to shorten its hours to save money. But most of the museum's revenue comes from evening events, which the shorter hours would eliminate. Cutting hours would therefore likely reduce the museum's overall income, not improve its finances.
items.Stem: Which one of the following most accurately states the conclusion of the argument?
items.ChoiceA: The museum is currently in serious financial trouble.
items.ChoiceB: The museum should hold more evening events.
items.ChoiceC: Cutting the museum's hours would likely reduce its overall income.
items.ChoiceD: The museum plans to shorten its hours.
items.ChoiceE: Most of the museum's revenue comes from evening events.
items.WhyWrongA: Not stated; the argument is about the effect of a specific plan.
items.WhyWrongB: The author never recommends this; out of scope.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. The last sentence is the author's judgment that the plan backfires.
items.WhyWrongD: This is the plan being evaluated, not the author's conclusion.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Many people believe that reading speed is fixed by a person's natural ability. In fact, studies show that targeted practice can substantially increase how fast someone reads with no loss of comprehension. Thus, reading speed is a skill that can be trained, not merely an innate trait.
items.Stem: Which one of the following most accurately expresses the main conclusion of the argument?
items.ChoiceA: Targeted practice can increase reading speed without loss of comprehension.
items.ChoiceB: Anyone can become a champion speed-reader with enough practice.
items.ChoiceC: Many people believe that reading speed is fixed by natural ability.
items.ChoiceD: Reading speed is a skill that can be trained rather than merely an innate trait.
items.ChoiceE: Comprehension matters more than speed when reading.
items.WhyWrongA: This is the supporting evidence, not the main point.
items.WhyWrongB: Too strong; the argument claims trainability, not champion-level results for all.
items.WhyWrongC: This is the common belief the author argues against, not the conclusion.
items.WhyWrongD: CORRECT. The 'Thus' sentence states the author's overall claim that speed is trainable.
items.WhyWrongE: A comparison the argument never makes.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The company argues that remote work lowers productivity. Yet its own data show that remote employees complete more projects per quarter than in-office staff do. The claim that remote work reduces productivity is therefore not supported by the company's own evidence.
items.Stem: The main point of the argument is that
items.ChoiceA: remote work is always more productive than working in an office.
items.ChoiceB: remote employees complete more projects per quarter than in-office staff.
items.ChoiceC: the company's own evidence does not support the claim that remote work reduces productivity.
items.ChoiceE: the company should require all of its employees to work remotely.
items.WhyWrongA: Too strong; the author says only that the claim is unsupported.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. The final sentence states the author's judgment about the evidence.
items.WhyWrongD: This is the claim the author rebuts, not the conclusion.
items.WhyWrongE: A recommendation the author never makes.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Some argue that we should ban cars from the downtown core to cut pollution. But most downtown pollution comes from a nearby factory, not from traffic. Banning cars would inconvenience thousands of residents while barely improving air quality. The proposed ban is thus not an effective way to reduce downtown pollution.
items.Stem: Which one of the following most accurately states the conclusion of the argument?
items.ChoiceA: Most downtown pollution comes from a nearby factory.
items.ChoiceB: The nearby factory should be shut down immediately.
items.ChoiceC: The proposed car ban is not an effective way to reduce downtown pollution.
items.ChoiceD: Banning cars would inconvenience thousands of residents.
items.ChoiceE: Cars should be banned from the downtown core.
items.WhyWrongA: A premise about the real source of pollution.
items.WhyWrongB: A recommendation the author does not make.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. The final sentence delivers the author's verdict on the proposal.
items.WhyWrongD: A premise about the ban's cost to residents.
items.WhyWrongE: This is the proposal the author argues against.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Editorial: Opponents of the new library tax say it burdens homeowners. However, the tax amounts to less than a dollar a month for the average household, and it will fund programs that thousands of children rely on. Clearly, the modest cost of the tax is well justified by its benefits.
items.Stem: Which one of the following most accurately expresses the main conclusion of the editorial?
items.ChoiceA: Opponents of the tax do not care about children.
items.ChoiceB: The new library tax burdens homeowners.
items.ChoiceC: The tax will fund programs that thousands of children rely on.
items.ChoiceD: The tax costs less than a dollar a month for the average household.
items.ChoiceE: The modest cost of the library tax is well justified by its benefits.
items.WhyWrongA: Too strong; the editorial never impugns opponents' motives.
items.WhyWrongB: This is the opponents' view, which the editorial rebuts.
items.WhyWrongC: A premise about the tax's benefits.
items.WhyWrongD: A premise about the tax's small size.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. The final sentence states the editorial's overall judgment.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Because the new drug reduced symptoms in nearly all trial patients, some conclude it should be approved at once. But the trial lasted only four weeks, and the drug's long-term effects are unknown. Approving it now would expose patients to unstudied risks. Regulators should therefore wait for longer-term data before approving the drug.
items.Stem: Which one of the following most accurately states the main conclusion of the argument?
items.ChoiceA: Regulators should wait for longer-term data before approving the drug.
items.ChoiceB: The drug is unsafe and should never be approved.
items.ChoiceC: The drug should be approved at once.
items.ChoiceD: Approving the drug now would expose patients to unstudied risks.
items.ChoiceE: The new drug reduced symptoms in nearly all trial patients.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. The 'therefore' sentence states the recommendation the argument builds toward.
items.WhyWrongB: Too strong; the author urges waiting, not a permanent ban.
items.WhyWrongC: The position the author argues against.
items.WhyWrongD: A supporting sub-claim, not the final recommendation.
items.WhyWrongE: A premise about the trial's short-term results.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Historian: It is often said that the empire fell because of foreign invasions. But the invasions succeeded only because the empire had already been weakened by decades of internal corruption and economic decline. The true cause of the collapse, then, lay within the empire itself.
items.Stem: The main point of the historian's argument is that
items.ChoiceA: the true cause of the empire's collapse lay within the empire itself.
items.ChoiceB: decades of corruption and economic decline weakened the empire.
items.ChoiceC: foreign invasions played no role whatsoever in the empire's fall.
items.ChoiceD: the empire fell because of foreign invasions.
items.ChoiceE: the invasions succeeded because the empire had already been weakened.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. The final sentence states the historian's overall thesis.
items.WhyWrongB: A premise about the source of that weakness.
items.WhyWrongC: Too strong; the historian grants the invasions a role, just not the root cause.
items.WhyWrongD: This is the common view the historian revises.
items.WhyWrongE: A premise linking the invasions to prior weakness.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Manager: We could cut costs by switching to the cheaper parts supplier. However, that supplier's parts fail far more often, and each failure halts our production line for hours. The savings on parts would be dwarfed by the cost of the resulting downtime. Switching suppliers would be a false economy.
items.Stem: Which one of the following most accurately expresses the main conclusion of the manager's argument?
items.ChoiceA: Switching to the cheaper supplier would reduce the company's parts costs.
items.ChoiceB: The cheaper supplier's parts fail far more often.
items.ChoiceC: Each parts failure halts the production line for hours.
items.ChoiceD: The company should never change any of its suppliers.
items.ChoiceE: Switching to the cheaper supplier would be a false economy.
items.WhyWrongA: A premise granting the apparent savings.
items.WhyWrongB: A premise about the parts' reliability.
items.WhyWrongC: A premise about the cost of failures.
items.WhyWrongD: Too strong; the argument concerns this one switch, not all suppliers.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. The final sentence states the manager's overall verdict on the switch.
